coredata.dc:title: Ginkgo biloba-derived biogenic carbon quantum dots modified NiCo-LDH: Significantly enhanced energy density and cycle stability
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lan, Yongzhi
Yu, Rui
Wang, Qing
Dai, Yile
Dai, Jianfeng
Qi zhang,
coredata.dc:description: A significant development in the usage of hydroxides in energy storage applications is the creation of LDH materials with excellent electrical conductivity and structural stability. Due to their poor electrical conductivity, bimetallic layered double hydroxides have weak rate performance and limited cycling ability. To address these issues, carbon quantum dots derived from waste Ginkgo biloba and labeled as GBC were incorporated in the preparation of NiCo-LDH. With the many oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, GBC increases the composite's wettability while maintaining the LDH lamellar structure. It can also create localized electron-rich regions, which give the material more space and channels for electron transport, improving electrical conductivity and rate performance. Furthermore, GBC is evenly dispersed throughout the LDH skeleton, giving the composites a homogenous surface state that can mitigate the structural collapse issue brought on by the volume change during cycling. The findings demonstrate that by modulating the amount of GBC, NiCo-LDH/GBC-20 performs better electrochemically than NiCo-LDH. The capacity was 276.1 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1, an 84.1 % improvement over NiCo-LDH. Finally, the energy density displayed by the NiCo-LDH/GBC-20//AC HSC device is 72.5 Wh kg-1 (at 798.7 W kg−1), and maintains 78.2 % of its original capacity after 12,000 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
NiCo-LDH
Ginkgo biloba
Carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow diagram for the NC-LDH/GBC preparation.
a XRD patterns of NC-LDH, NC-LDH/GBC-10, NC-LDH/GBC-20, and NC-LDH/GBC-30; b Raman spectra of NC-LDH/GBC.
a XPS total spectra. High-resolution spectroscopy of the b Ni 2p, c Co 2p, d O 1s, and e C 1s of NC-LDH/GBC-20.

SEM diagram of NC-LDH/GBC-10, NC-LDH/GBC-20, and NC-LDH/GBC-30 in different magnifications (a–c) and NC-LDH/GBC-10, NC-LDH/GBC-20, and NC-LDH/GBC-30 in different magnifications (d–f); (g-k) elemental distribution of NC-LDH/GBC-20.
TEM diagram of (a–c) NC-LDH/GBC-20 at different magnifications. d The high-resolution TEM picture of NC-LDH/GBC-20.

Comparison of (a) CV profiles at 50 mV s−1 and (b) GCD profiles at 1 A g−1 of the pure NC-LDH, NC-LDH/GBC-10, NC-LDH/GBC-20, and NC-LDH/GBC-30 composite electrodes. (c) CV curves at different scan rates and (d) GCD curves at different current densities of NC-LDH. (e) Rate capability. (f) Nyquist plots and (g) cycling stability of NC-LDH, NC-LDH/GBC-10, NC-LDH/GBC-20, and NC-LDH/GBC-30 under 5000 times.
a b value, b contribution of surface control of NC-LDH/GBC-20 (20 mV s−1). c Contribution of two different energy storage methods at 5–50 mV of NC-LDH/GBC-20.

a NC-LDH/GBC-20//AC HSC Device Schematics. b Positive and negative CV test results of NC-LDH/GBC-20//AC HSC devices. c CV test results at several voltage ranges; d CV test results at 5–50 mV s−1; e results of GCD tests at 1–20 A g−1; and f specific capacitance values at 1–20 A g−1 of the NC-LDH/GBC-20//AC HSC. g Ragone plot of the NC-LDH/GBC-20//AC HSC device compared with previously reported HSC devices [50–57]. h cycling stability of the NC-LDH/GBC-20//AC HSC device.

head.ce:title: Ginkgo biloba-derived biogenic carbon quantum dots modified NiCo-LDH: Significantly enhanced energy density and cycle stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, Gansu, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, Gansu, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Nonferrous Metals, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, Gansu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Nonferrous Metals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Nonferrous Metals, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, Gansu, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Science, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, Gansu, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A significant development in the usage of hydroxides in energy storage applications is the creation of LDH materials with excellent electrical conductivity and structural stability. Due to their poor electrical conductivity, bimetallic layered double hydroxides have weak rate performance and limited cycling ability. To address these issues, carbon quantum dots derived from waste Ginkgo biloba and labeled as GBC were incorporated in the preparation of NiCo-LDH. With the many oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, GBC increases the composite's wettability while maintaining the LDH lamellar structure. It can also create localized electron-rich regions, which give the material more space and channels for electron transport, improving electrical conductivity and rate performance. Furthermore, GBC is evenly dispersed throughout the LDH skeleton, giving the composites a homogenous surface state that can mitigate the structural collapse issue brought on by the volume change during cycling. The findings demonstrate that by modulating the amount of GBC, NiCo-LDH/GBC-20 performs better electrochemically than NiCo-LDH. The capacity was 276.1 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1, an 84.1 % improvement over NiCo-LDH. Finally, the energy density displayed by the NiCo-LDH/GBC-20//AC HSC device is 72.5 Wh kg-1 (at 798.7 W kg−1), and maintains 78.2 % of its original capacity after 12,000 cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The energy issue has been somewhat mitigated by the ongoing development of new energy, but its inherent erratic nature prevents new energy from becoming widely accepted [1,2]. Energy storage technology has long been prioritized and developed in an effort to overcome this challenging issue [3]. As a result, energy storage devices such as batteries and supercapacitors have become widely used. As technology advances, more emphasis has been placed on the needs of energy storage devices in terms of affordability, sustainability, safety, and environmental friendliness. Supercapacitors are preferred by researchers over batteries because of their high safety factor, quick charging and discharging, adaptability to most working environments, and capacity to produce large amounts of power quickly. It is not comparable to the energy density of batteries, though. Two approaches to addressing the low energy density issue are extending the voltage range over which the supercapacitor device operates and preparing electrode materials with high specific capacitance; the former can be accomplished by assembling hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) devices, while the latter necessitates the ongoing development of high-performance materials [4–13].

HSC devices currently assembled with battery-type electrode materials have a large capacity and can provide a large energy density, which is perfectly suitable for research needs. Transition metal oxides [14–17], sulfides [18,19], phosphides [20,21], and hydroxides [22–24], for instance, are popular materials for electrochemical energy storage because of their profusion of redox states. Hydroxides, in particular, have become the material of choice for most researchers due to their unique layered structure, controllability of metal ions, and synergistic effects between different metal ions [25,26]. For instance, Dighe et al. used a hydrothermal process to synthesize NiCo-LDH on nickel foam, achieving a specific capacitance of 175.2 mAh g−1 at 1 mA cm2 [27]; Bao et al. synthesized flower-like CoNi LDH NFs by the solvothermal method, achieving a capacity of 213.4 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 [28]; and Zou et al. prepared honeycomb Ni–Co LDH/G composites with a capacity of 175.1 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 [29]. These investigations into LDH have, however, brought to light some flaws in the material, including low mechanical stability, low electrical conductivity, and poor cycle ability, all of which greatly affect its development. Therefore, finding a material with strong electrical conductivity and mechanical strength to compound with LDH is an ideal solution.

CQDs have been very hot at the moment as a quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterial with an extremely small size that can easily enter the structure of other materials and hardly affect their structure, in addition to providing space and channels for electron transport inside the material and showing good electrochemical activity, both of which will undoubtedly improve the precursor material's rate properties and electrical conductivity [30–37]. Additionally, the incorporation of CQDs can enhance the material's structural stability, mitigate volume collapse during several cycles, and increase long-term cycle performance [38–43]. There are many applications of CQDs in energy storage as a result. Sahoo et al. synthesized CQDs-doped hierarchical flower-like nickel sulfide materials using a hydrothermal method to improve the charge transfer process by taking advantage of the good electronic and surface properties of CQDs, which have a specific capacity of 122.2 mAh g−1 at 2A g−1 [44]; Ji et al. hydrothermally created three-dimensional layered flower-like structures out of nickel phosphide, nitrogen-doped CQDs, and cobalt oxide. It has a capacity of 290 mAh g−1 and 243.3 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 20 A g−1 [45]. The capacitance of the CQDs/Ni-MOF composite, which was created by Pan et al. using a one-pot hydrothermal technique, was 341.4 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1. The capacitance was maintained at 84.5 % after 5000 cycles of charging and discharging, exhibiting remarkable cyclic stability [40]. These investigations have shown how important CQDs are for improving a material's structural stability, rate qualities, and electrical conductivity [46–48].

However, the cost of CQDs prepared through chemicals is very high, so it is imperative to explore biomass-derived CQDs, which not only control the cost but also have the characteristics of ordinary CQDs, making them an ideal additive for electrode materials. Biomass can be mainly extracted from plants, agricultural wastes, animals, algae, and bacteria; for example, Baslak et al. synthesized CQDs with a size of 10 nm by using fermented tea as a raw material and a carbon source and confirmed that CQDs have unique redox properties and high capacitance effects, which can be compounded with other electrode materials and exhibit excellent electrochemical properties [49]. Wang et al. used cottonseed meal as raw material to prepare CQDs, which, after composite with porous carbon, had an ultra-high specific capacitance of 477 F g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 [50]. All these studies provide ideas for the application of biomass-derived CQDs.

In this work, a low-cost, environmentally friendly approach was used to synthesize GBC from waste Ginkgo biloba. Compared to the introduction of other elemental doping, GBC obtained by the hydrothermal method using Ginkgo biloba as a raw material has a low acquisition cost and simple preparation. The LDH materials were then created by varying the doped GBC concentration while Ni2+ hydrolysis etching MOF was carried out. NiCo-LDH/GBC with different doping levels were obtained with desirable electrochemical properties, and as expected, the prepared NiCo-LDH/GBC-20 (NC-LDH/GBC-20) exhibited superior electrochemical properties, which has a significant capacity of 276.1 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1. The energy density of the HSC devices composed of NC-LDH/GBC-20 as the anode material and activated carbon (AC) as the anode material was 72.5 Wh kg−1, and the capacitance retention rate after 12000 cycles of charging and discharging was 78.2 %.

To create the red solution A, 30 mL of methanol solution was first mixed with 3 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and the mixture was continuously agitated for 30 min. Subsequently, 90 mL of methanol solution were mixed quickly with 12 mmol of 2-methylimidazo for 30 min, resulting in the clear and colorless solution B. Ultimately, B was rapidly added to A, and the combined purple solution was constantly agitated for 30 min. Subsequent resting for 48 h. After extracting the purple product at 4000 rpm, the precipitate was cleaned three times using methanol and deionized water before being vacuum-dried for 24 h at 60 °C in a drying oven. The sample that was eventually obtained was called Co-MOF, and the sample that was ultimately obtained was termed Co-MOF.

Some discarded Gingo biloba leaves were collected, washed, and placed in a constant-temperature oven at 60 °C for drying. An appropriate amount of well-dried leaves was taken and ground into powder. Add 2 g of this powder in 50 mL of water, and after sufficient mixing and sonicating for 20 min, the resulting green solution was hydrothermalized at 200 °C for 10 h, and after natural cooling, the brown solution obtained from the reaction was centrifuged to remove the precipitate. The solution obtained was the GBC solution, and in order to further remove the impurities in the GBC solution, the resulting solution was subjected to vacuum filtration with a filter pore size of 0.22 μm in the filter paper, and finally the GBC solution was refrigerated at 4 °C.

Beaker A was filled with 100 mg of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 10 mL of the previously mentioned GBC brown solution, and 20 mL of ethanol and stirred thoroughly. During stirring, 200 mg of Co-MOF was gradually added to it. The obtained mixed solution was continuously heated at 60 °C for 12 h for sufficient Ni2+ hydrolysis etching. Finally, the solution at the end of the reaction was centrifuged, and the precipitate was collected, washed, and dried. The light green powder obtained was named NC-LDH/GBC-10. To find the optimal ratio of added GBC solution, the amount of added GBC solution (20 mL and 30 mL) was varied to serve as a control group, named NC-LDH/GBC-20 and NC-LDH/GBC-30.

Fig. 1 displays the schematic diagram of the NC-LDH/GBC composites' production process. Initially, Co-MOF was created using the standard co-precipitation technique of 2-methylimidazo and Co(NO3)2·6H2O in methanol. Next, NC-LDH/GBC materials were prepared by Ni2+ hydrolysis and hydrothermal technique using Co-MOF as a precursor, Ni(NO3)2–6H2O as a nickel source, and waste Ginkgo biloba as a carbon source.

XRD scanning was done to more thoroughly examine the impact of adding 10 mL, 20 mL, and 30 mL of GBC on the material's crystal structure. As in Fig. 2 a, different diffraction peaks for the 2θ values of the NC-LDH/GBC electrode can be seen at 11.2°, 22.8°, 34°, and 60.7°; these align with LDH's crystal planes (003), (006), (009), and (110) [34]. This outcome shows that the samples were prepared correctly and that there was no significant effect on the structure of LDH after the addition of GBC. It is also noted that the position of the typical diffraction peaks belonging to the (003) crystal plane is shifted by a small angle (leftward) with respect to the pure LDH, suggesting that there are some defects in the NiCo-LDH after the doping of GBC; it may also be due to the larger layer spacing caused by the doping of GBC. The (003) crystal planes of NC-LDH and NC-LDH/GBC-20 were tested to correspond to 12.7° and 11.2°, respectively. According to
2 d
sin
θ = n λ
, the crystal pitch of NC-LDH/GBC-20 is 0.038 nm larger than that of NC-LDH. Meanwhile, three composites incorporating GBC can be found to have distinct carbon peaks at 15°–30° with respect to NiCo-LDH, indicating the presence of GBC. The disordered D-band and the crystalline G-band are the two typical peaks seen in the Raman spectra of carbon materials. The Raman spectra of the three composites in Fig. 2b show the disordered D-band, which is approximately 1325 cm−1, and the graphitized sp2 carbon G-band, which is approximately 1573 cm−1. After the addition of GBC, the calculated ID/IG values are all below 1, indicating that the composites are highly graphitized, which helps to improve their electrical conductivity and electrochemical activity. Meanwhile, the metal hydroxide peak at 526 cm−1 can be clearly observed.

XPS spectroscopy was utilized to assess the chemical makeup and valence of NC-LDH/GBC-20. The spectra in Fig. 3 a indicate that the samples were composed of Ni, Co, O, and C components. The HRXPS plot of Ni 2p (Fig. 3b) has two peaks at 855.75 and 873.30 eV matching the +3 valence Ni element and peaks at 857.20 and 875.25 eV corresponding to the +2 valence Ni element. The Ni 2p spectra also contained two satellite peaks at 879.98 and 861.76 eV. The presence of Ni2+ and Ni3+ in the electrode material was verified by these findings. As shown in Fig. 3c, two binding energies in the XPS spectra of Co at 780.90 and 796.6 eV are associated with Co3+, while another pair at 782.50 and 798.07 eV is associated with Co2+. Also, two satellite peaks at 786.58 and 802.75 eV indicate that the sample has both +2 and + 3 valence cobalt ions. As shown in Fig. 3d, the HRXPS spectrum of O 1s by the Gaussian method produced three peaks at 532.50, 531.25, and 530.50 eV, representing M-O-M, M-O-H, and H–O–H, respectively. The spectra of C1s are displayed in Fig. 3d. The peaks at 288.42, 284.60, and 286.10 eV correspond to CO, CC/C–C, and C–O bonds, respectively. These findings provide more evidence of the successful synthesis of NC-LDH/GBC.

SEM was used to investigate the samples' surface morphology, and various scanning magnifications were used to describe the micro-morphology of the NC-LDH/GBC materials. Fig. 4 a–b illustrates that, despite the presence of some agglomerations and collapses, the overall distribution of NC-LDH/GBC-10 and NC-LDH/GBC-20 is relatively homogeneous. Meanwhile, Fig. 4d–e shows that, through high magnification electron microscope shots, the as-prepared NC-LDH/GBC-10 and NC-LDH/GBC-20 electrode materials still retain the fundamental dodecahedral framework of Co-MOF, and the surfaces exhibit a clear crosslinked nanosheet layer structure, which gives the material more reactive sites and enhances the materials' specific capacitance during charging and discharging. The GBC was too tiny to be seen in the SEM picture. With the increase in the amount of GBC, it was found that the morphology of LDH changed significantly. As shown in Fig. 4c and f, when GBC was added up to 30 mL, the surface of the prepared NC-LDH/GBC-30 changed significantly. Instead of the laminar structure of nanosheets, the surface consisted of nanoscale microspheres of much smaller sizes, which led to degradation of the specific surface area and capacity. The surface distribution of the elements was further evaluated by EDS. The four elements—Co, Ni, O, and C—are discovered to be evenly dispersed throughout the material's surface, as shown by Fig. 4g–k, which further demonstrates the success of the preparation of NC-LDH/GBC.

The transmission morphology of NiCo-LDH composite GBC based on MOF is examined in greater detail through TEM images, as shown in Fig. 5 a–c. It is evident from these images that the prepared NC-LDH/GBC-20 still maintains the fundamental morphology of the ZIF-67 regular polyhedra, which is consistent with the results in SEM. Additionally, it is intuitive. The TEM pictures demonstrate that the material's middle and edge thicknesses are noticeably different, which adds more evidence that the material's interior is hollow. At the same time, the lamellar structure of the surface can be clearly observed, and this unique structure with obvious lamellae on the surface as well as internal hollowing has more electrochemical reaction sites, which can increase the contact with the electrolyte during the reaction process. Lattice fringes that matched the LDH crystal planes (003) and (009) were measured, with spacings of d = 0.13 and 0.19 nm. Furthermore, the carbon (100) surface is matched to d = 0.21 nm, and the inset in Fig. 5d further illustrates the amorphous character of the composite, which is consistent with the XRD findings.

To get a deeper comprehension of the electrochemical properties of four distinct composites, NC-LDH, NC-LDH/GBC-10, NC-LDH/GBC-20, and NC-LDH/GBC-30, the electrochemical properties of each were measured using a three-electrode setup in a 3 M aqueous KOH solution. The four distinct composite CV curves are displayed in Fig. 6 a at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 within a voltage range of −0.2–0.6 V. The CV curves display two separate redox reaction peaks. It was found that the NC-LDH/GBC-20 CV curve encompasses the largest area, indicating that the LDH composite performs better electrochemically than the other composites when 20 mL of GBC is compounded. GBC has good electrical conductivity, and the composite will improve the electron transport ability of the whole electrode material. The addition of different proportions of GBC will change the electron transport rate and path in the electrode material. When the proportion of GBC is moderate, a good conductive network will be formed, which promotes the rapid transfer of electrons to the active sites in NC-LDH where oxidation reactions occur, so that the oxidation reaction is more likely to take place and the oxidation peak potential may be shifted in the direction of the low point position. In contrast, when the ratio of GBC is too high or too low, the formation of the conductive network is not ideal, which affects the efficiency of electron transfer and leads to changes in the oxidation peak potential. The GCD curves of four distinct composites are evaluated at 1 A g−1 in Fig. 6b. During the charging and discharging process, the GCD curves have obvious charging and discharging plateau potentials, reflecting the battery-type characteristics of the NC-LDH/GBC materials. Based on the GCD curves, it was found that adjusting the added amount of GBC helped to enhance the capacity of the LDH. The GCD curve, in particular, attained its greatest value with the longest charging and discharging time when 20 mL GBC was added. This meant that the NC-LDH/GBC-20 material had the largest capacitance, with a specific capacity of up to 276.1 mAh g−1.

The CV test results of NC-LDH/GBC-20 at various scan rates, ranging from 10 to 50 mV s−1, are displayed in Fig. 6c. When the scan rate is increased, the redox peaks shift to high and low potentials, respectively, while the curves' shape stays the same. This suggests that the materials have good reversibility, which is mostly because the redox-active materials have low ohmic resistance and ionic diffusion resistance. The GCD test results for NC-LDH/GBC-20 at various current densities are shown in Fig. 6d. The voltage plateaus in these curves match the redox peaks in the CV curves, which together demonstrate the good reversibility of the material. Based on the GCD curves, the specific capacities of the electrodes calculated by Eq. (S1) were 276.1, 267.3, 260.5, 250.3, 230.6, and 201.7 mAh g−1, respectively. Combined with the rate performance graph in Fig. 6e, it is intuitively clear that NC-LDH/GBC-20 has better rate performance and is more suitable for use under extreme conditions. The Nyquist plots for the four distinct composite electrodes in the 0.01–106 Hz frequency range are displayed in Fig. 6f. All electrodes have essentially identical electrochemical impedance spectra, with straight lines in the low-frequency region and semicircular curves in the high-frequency region.

The series resistance (Rs) is represented by the high-frequency portion's intercept, and the diameter of the semicircle is then used to evaluate the charge transfer resistance (Rct). The NC-LDH/GBC-20 electrode exhibited the least Rs (0.57 Ω) and Rct (0.28 Ω) values when the EIS curves for the four materials were fitted. This indicates that it has a faster electronic transfer mechanism. When the impedance is fitted in accordance with the equivalent circuit schematic in Fig. 6f, furthermore, the line's slope represents Warburg diffusion resistance (ZW).The greater the slope of the NC-LDH/GBC-20 electrode curve, the steeper it is; this indicates a lower diffusion resistance and an increased capacity for ion diffusion. At 10 A g−1, the capacitance held at 94 % after 5000 cycles (Fig. 6g). The loss of electrode material during the continuous charging process might be the cause of the capacitance deterioration. When GBC was added, the material's stability and conductivity both increased dramatically.

Enhanced comprehension of the electrode material's response kinetics via
i = a
v b
. The b-value is calculated from the oxidation peak and reduction peak; according to the size of the b-value, we can judge the contribution of different energy storage modes to the specific capacitance, and the b-value of the NC-LDH/GBC-20 electrode is 0.71 and 0.55, respectively. This implies that pseudocapacitive (diffusion-controlled) and capacitive (surface-controlled) processes make up the charge storage mechanism (Fig. 7 a). The contribution share of the two different control processes is calculated by the following equation:
(1)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2

Where i denotes the current density, k 1 and k 2 are constants, and v is the scan rate of the CV curve. In Fig. 7b, using the CV curve of 20 mV s−1 as an example, 38 % of the specific capacitance of NC-LDH/GBC-20 comes from the capacitive contribution, and its CV shape exceeds the original curve due to the influence of polarization. This representation intuitively demonstrates the fast electrochemical kinetics in supercapacitors, and the percentage of the capacitive contribution gradually increases when the scan rate is increased to 50 mV s−1 and finally reaches 74 % (Fig. 7c), which may be due to the fact that it takes some time for the electrolyte to diffuse into the interior of the electrode material at large scan rates. Therefore, at 50 mV s−1, the capacitive contribution dominates.

In a 2 M aqueous potassium hydroxide solution, NC-LDH/GBC-20 and AC were employed as the positive and negative materials for supercapacitors. Fig. 8 a illustrates the construction of this hybrid NC-LDH/GBC-20//AC device. Whereas, Fig. 8b demonstrates the CV curves of AC and NC-LDH/GBC-20 electrodes in a three-electrode system at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 with an extended voltage range of −1.0–0.6 V for the HSC device. The CV test results of NC-LDH/GBC-20//AC in various voltage windows at 50 mV s−1 are displayed in Fig. 8c. It is evident that the HSC device struggles to adjust to the operating environment below the 0–1.8 V window, and the polarization of the CV curve is clearly visible. The shape of the curve is close to rectangular at 0–1.6V and smaller voltage windows, proving that the maximum operating voltage range of this HSC device is 0–1.6V. The HSC device was next put through CV testing inside this window at various scan rates (Fig. 8d). Every curve retains a quasi-rectangular form, confirming that the device's stable operating window is between 0 and 1.6 V.

Next, GCD tests were performed on the HSC device to assess its electrochemical performance. The GCD curves, as shown in Fig. 8e, exhibit a distinct symmetry at different current densities, demonstrating the device's superior electrochemical performance. The reason for the asymmetry of the CV and GCD curves is that in the two-electrode system of a double-layer capacitor, the CV curve usually has a more ideal rectangular shape. The CV curve of a hybrid supercapacitor, on the other hand, usually exhibits a rectangular-like shape with a certain degree of distortion compared to the ideal rectangular CV curve of a pure double-electric layer capacitor. This is because one of the electrodes of the hybrid supercapacitor is dominated by the double layer capacitance behavior (AC electrode), which stores charge mainly through the adsorption and detachment of ions on the electrode surface during charging and discharging, and its CV curve partially exhibits the characteristics of the double layer capacitance, which is close to a rectangular shape. The other electrode is dominated by pseudocapacitance behavior (NC-LDH/GBC-20), which undergoes a rapid redox reaction during the charging and discharging process, generating a Faraday current, which causes the CV curve to deviate from the rectangular shape at specific potential intervals and thus will not be completely symmetrical. The GCD curves of hybrid supercapacitors usually show an approximate triangular shape, and the AC electrode has a better reaction reversibility, with more rapid and reversible ion adsorption and desorption reactions during charging and discharging, whereas the NC-LDH/GBC-20 electrode has a relatively poorer reversibility of the redox reaction. In forward charging and reverse discharging processes, the rate and degree of oxidation and reduction reactions may be inconsistent, resulting in asymmetric GCD curves. Furthermore, the HSC device's specific capacitance values were obtained by their discharge timings. At current densities of 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, and 20 A g−1, the HSC's capacity values were 204.0, 160.9, 143.3, 127.2, 108.1, and 85.0 F g−1 (Fig. 8f), respectively. As seen in Fig. 8f, electrode polarization and insufficient charging and discharging processes cause the HSC's specific capacitance to decrease as current density rises.

Fig. 8g displays the energy and power densities of the HSC device based on the findings displayed in the Ragone plot. The data indicates that the HSC surpasses previously reported LDH-based HSC devices (Table S1) [50–57], reaching a maximum energy density of 72.5 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 798.7 W kg−1 and maintaining an energy density of 30.2 Wh kg−1 even at 16,000 W kg−1. Additionally, a lengthy cycle life is required to guarantee the electrochemical stability of the HSC device, as seen in Fig. 8h, where after 12,000 cycles of cyclic charging and discharging, the specific capacitance of NC-LDH/GBC-20//AC HSCs stays at 75 % of the original specific capacitance.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized NC-LDH/GBC-20 electrodes by a simultaneous composite of GBC on the basis of Ni2+ hydrolytically etched MOF and optimized the amount of waste Ginkgo biloba-derived GBC added during the preparation process, which allowed us to successfully construct the hollow cage material that maintains a large pore MOF structure and has a large specific surface area, maximizing the transition metal ions between them and the synergistic effect between carbon materials. Consequently, the specific capacities of the NC-LDH/GBC-20 electrode are 276.1 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 201.75 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1. Moreover, the HSC device assembled with NC-LDH/GBC-20 as the anode and AC exhibits good cycling stability and a high energy density of 72.5 Wh kg−1, demonstrating excellent electrochemical performance. Consequently, the sensible addition of GBC materials is a useful strategy to enhance the stability and energy density of supercapacitors.

Yongzhi Lan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rui Yu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Qing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yile Dai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jianfeng Dai: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Qi zhang: Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Hong Liu First-class Disciplines Development Program of Lanzhou University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2024.130135.
